title: Wet-felted wreath
subtitle: A felted wool wreath base, 25 cm diameter, worked on a rope core
excerpt: A wreath base made by wrapping merino roving tightly around a natural-fibre rope ring and wet-felting until firm. The rope provides the core shape; no resist is needed. Finished diameter is 25 cm. Needle-felted details can be added once dry.
sourceNotes: Rope-core wet-felted wreaths are a common seasonal craft project in UK fibre arts circles. Technique synthesised from general wet-felting and rope-core wrapping practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

glossaryTerms.definition: Combed and drawn wool fibres in a continuous rope, ready for wet felting or spinning.
The final agitation stage of wet felting in which fibres are compressed and shrunk by rubbing, rolling, and throwing, producing a dense, firm fabric.
A technique in which roving or yarn is wound tightly around a pre-formed core (rope, wire, foam) before felting, so the core holds the structure while the wool bonds around it.

content.text: A wet-felted wreath base is built on a natural-fibre rope ring. Sisal or jute rope is formed into a circle and tied securely, then
roving
is wound tightly around the rope using the
core-wrapping
technique. Once the rope is fully covered, the wrapped ring is wet-felted and
fulled

until the wool grips the rope and forms a firm, uniform surface. The finished wreath is 25 cm in diameter. After drying, it can be decorated with needle-felted foliage, berries, or any dry embellishment secured with wire or glue. No resist is needed because the rope provides the permanent structure.

items.name: Natural-fibre rope: sisal or jute, 1-1.5 cm diameter
Twine or cable tie for joining rope ends

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the rope ring

Cut 85 cm of rope. Form it into a ring and join the two ends together: either tie with twine so the join is tight and the ends do not protrude, or use a cable tie cinched flat. The ring should measure 25 cm in diameter when laid flat. Natural fibre ropes are preferred over synthetic because the wool fibres grip them more reliably.
Step 2: Wrap the rope with roving

Pull a length of roving about 2 cm wide. Starting at the join, wrap the roving tightly around the rope in a spiral, overlapping each round by half its width. Wind firmly enough that the rope is completely hidden and the roving does not shift when the ring is handled. Join lengths of roving by overlapping the new end on top of the previous end by about 5 cm and continuing to wrap over the overlap. When the entire ring is covered, tuck the final end under a previous wrap and press flat.
Step 3: Wet down

Mix olive-oil soap into warm water (about 40°C) in a bowl large enough to submerge the ring. Submerge the wrapped ring and squeeze gently so the soapy water penetrates through the roving wrapping to the rope core. Lift out and hold over the bowl to let excess water drip. The ring should feel heavy and fully saturated.
Step 4: Rub and felt

Place the wet ring on the felting mat. Working around the ring, rub the outer surface firmly with both palms in a rolling motion, turning the ring as you go. Continue for 10 minutes. Then pick up the ring and rub sections between your palms, squeezing inward toward the rope core. The roving should begin to knit together and grip the rope. Dip the ring back in soapy water every few minutes to keep it warm and slippery.
Step 5: Roll in the bamboo mat

Place the ring on the bamboo mat and roll the mat around it. Roll back and forth for 5 minutes, then open the mat, rotate the ring, and roll again. Repeat three or four times. The wrapping should no longer shift or slide on the rope.
Step 6: Full until firm

Continue rubbing the ring between your palms with increasing pressure, working around the full circumference. Throw the ring onto the mat 10 times. Check that the wool surface is uniform with no gaps or thin spots. If any bare rope is visible, add a small length of roving over the gap, wet it, and rub it in. The finished surface should be dense and slightly fuzzy, with the rope core solid but not visible.
Step 7: Rinse and dry

Rinse the ring in water at the same temperature as the wash water (30-40°C) until the water runs clear. Squeeze firmly to remove excess water. Hang the ring to dry, or lay flat on a towel. The rope core will take several hours to dry through completely. Do not decorate the wreath until it is fully dry.

items.problem: The roving wrapping slides around the rope and gaps appear
items.cause: The roving was not wound tightly enough in Step 2, or it was applied too loosely over the rope
items.fix: Re-wet and re-rub the gap area, pressing the wool firmly inward. In future, wrap with slightly more tension and ensure each round overlaps the previous one by half.

items.problem: The rope join is visible as a lump through the felt
items.cause: The join was too thick or the roving was not wound over it with sufficient coverage
items.fix: Apply extra roving over the join area. Wet it and rub directly over the lump until it blends into the surrounding surface.

items.problem: The finished ring is more oval than circular
items.cause: The rope was not joined at equal lengths, or uneven rubbing distorted the ring during felting
items.fix: Check the ring diameter at multiple points before joining the rope ends. During fulling, work around the circumference evenly rather than concentrating on one section.

content.text: Care and finishing

The wreath base is intended as a decorating surface rather than a wearable item, so it does not need washing. Dust lightly with a soft brush. If used outdoors, bring inside in wet weather to protect both the rope core and any added embellishments. Needle-felted additions can be worked directly into the surface; push the felting needle through the wool into the rope core and pull back out carefully to avoid snapping the needle.
